title: CRUD Fundamentals
description: Create, Read, Update, and Delete documents. Map every SQL DML statement to its MongoDB equivalent.

sections.content: MongoDB gives you two insert methods: `insertOne()` for a single document and `insertMany()` for a batch. Both return an `acknowledged` result with the inserted `_id`(s).

sections.heading: insertOne — add a single document
insertMany — batch insert

sections.content: **Ordered vs unordered inserts:** By default, `insertMany` is **ordered** — it stops on the first error. Pass `{ ordered: false }` to continue inserting remaining docs even if one fails. PostgreSQL has no direct equivalent; it always rolls back the whole statement on error.
The `find()` method is the `SELECT` of MongoDB. Pass a **filter object** as the first argument (the WHERE clause), and an optional **projection** as the second argument (the column list).

sections.heading: find() — basic queries
findOne() — return just the first match

sections.content: **Cursors:** `find()` returns a **cursor** (lazy iterator), not all results at once. In mongosh, results are shown automatically. In Node.js code, call `.toArray()` to get all results, or iterate the cursor with `for await (const doc of cursor)`.
**Projection gotcha:** You cannot mix includes (1) and excludes (0) in the same projection — except for `_id` which can always be excluded with `_id: 0`.

MongoDB has several update methods. The key concept is **update operators** — these are special keywords starting with `$` that tell MongoDB *how* to change the document, not just *what value* to set.

sections.heading: The most important update operators
UPDATE vs updateOne/updateMany
table: UPDATE books SET price = 14.99 WHERE title = '1984';
UPDATE books SET price = price + 2 WHERE id = 1;

sections.content: **Never do this:** `db.books.updateOne({ title: '1984' }, { price: 15 })` — without an update operator like `$set`, MongoDB **replaces the entire document** with `{ price: 15 }`. All other fields are lost! Always use `$set` to update specific fields.

sections.heading: upsert — insert if not found (like INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE)
deleteOne and deleteMany

sections.content: **countDocuments()** counts matching documents: `db.books.countDocuments({ inStock: true })` — equivalent to `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM books WHERE in_stock = true`.

**find() returns a cursor, not an array.** A cursor is a lazy iterator — it does not load all documents into memory at once. In mongosh, the shell automatically iterates and displays results. In Node.js code, you must consume the cursor explicitly.

sections.heading: Consuming a cursor in Node.js

sections.content: **findOneAndUpdate** vs **updateOne** — these are different in what they return. `updateOne` returns metadata (matchedCount, modifiedCount). `findOneAndUpdate` returns the actual document — either before or after the update.

sections.heading: findOneAndUpdate — get the document back
Returning the document after update
table: UPDATE books SET price=15.99 WHERE title='1984' RETURNING *;

sections.content: **insertMany is faster than looping insertOne.** When you need to insert many documents, always use `insertMany` — it sends all documents in a single network request. A loop of 1000 `insertOne` calls sends 1000 separate network round trips.

**Ordered vs unordered insertMany.** By default, `insertMany` is ordered — it stops at the first error (e.g. a duplicate key). Pass `{ ordered: false }` to continue inserting remaining documents even if one fails.

sections.heading: ordered: false — don't stop on error
sections.content:
db.books.insertMany(
  [doc1, doc2, doc3],
  { ordered: false }   // doc3 inserts even if doc2 fails
)

**Pagination with skip() has a performance problem.** `.skip(1000).limit(10)` makes MongoDB read and discard 1000 documents before returning 10 — this gets slower as the page number increases. The better pattern is range-based pagination using the last seen `_id`.

sections.heading: Range-based pagination (fast) vs skip-based (slow)

sections.content: **The skip() trap:** Page 1 is fast, page 10 is OK, page 500 is very slow. This is the same problem as `OFFSET 10000` in PostgreSQL — both databases have to walk through and discard all skipped rows/documents.
